one_line_takeaway: Invisible backdoor triggers largely die under realistic lossy image compression because they rely on high-frequency information that codecs discard, but attackers can use Region-of-Interest coding to either reactivate existing triggers or design new triggers (CAA) that the compression pipeline itself induces, achieving near-100% attack success and strong robustness to defenses and re-compression.
key_finding: Under standard lossy compression, invisible/clean-label attacks lose most effectiveness (e.g., FTrojan ASR on CIFAR-10/ResNet18 drops to 12.53%, BppAttack to 44.17%, WaNet to 77.58%, versus their much higher uncompressed ASRs; clean-label SAA and Narcissus fall to 10.04% and 17.27% ASR on GTSRB/ResNet18). UAR reactivation recovers most of this loss (e.g., FTrojanReA reaches 94.91% ASR on CIFAR-10/ResNet18, a +82.38 point gain; WaNetReA reaches 98.43%; BppAttackReA reaches 96.23%), and also reactivates compression-aware attacks ECA (23.20% to 95.10% ASR on CIFAR-10) and CBA (27.10% to 98.20%). CAA, the compression-native attack, achieves >99.9% ASR on all four backbones across CIFAR-10/GTSRB/CelebA in the all-to-one setting with BA comparable to clean models, and near-100% ASR (93.76-99.46%) in the harder all-to-one/all-to-all settings, and consistently near-100% ASR across all four tested codecs (JPEG2000, BPG, Cheng-2020, ELIC) on CIFAR-10. CAA also resists Fine-Pruning, STRIP, Gaussian blur/noise defenses, and (unlike FTrojanReA, which nearly fails) survives JPEG re-compression, achieving ASR above 95% at default/high re-compression quality with a sparse ROI mask. Stealthiness metrics show CAA (PSNR 37.4, SSIM 0.965, 52.4% human-inspection detection rate) far exceeds visible-trigger BadNet (PSNR 21.76, SSIM 0.916, 8.3% detection).
core_contribution: The paper (title in the PDF is 'Backdoor Attacks under Lossy Compression: From Failure to Reactivation and Adaptation', arXiv:2603.13864) shows that invisible/clean-label backdoor triggers embedded in RGB images are frequently destroyed when poisoned datasets pass through a realistic lossy-compression storage-and-transmission pipeline (e.g., JPEG-style bitstreams), because such triggers rely on high-frequency components that lossy codecs suppress. The authors then propose two ROI (Region-of-Interest)-coding-based poisoning strategies that explicitly account for this pipeline: (1) Universal Attack Reactivation (UAR), which uses sample-specific residual- or frequency-derived ROI masks during learned image compression (LIC) to preserve/reactivate an existing attack's trigger information through compression, and (2) Compression-Adapted Attack (CAA), a new attack that encodes malicious information directly into the compressed bitstream via customized (e.g., checkerboard/concentric-square) ROI masks, applicable to both traditional codecs and LICs, so the trigger is induced by compression rather than destroyed by it.
threat_model: Dataset-only poisoning: the attacker releases a poisoned dataset (not a modified model or training pipeline) that must be stored/transmitted as a standard-compliant compressed bitstream before a victim decodes it and trains a model with an arbitrary standard training pipeline. Attacker capabilities: can poison images before compression and can configure/control the sender-side encoder (including ROI masks) using public codec specifications; cannot modify the victim's decoder, training algorithm, or model architecture, and cannot require a nonstandard decoder. Constraints: output bitstreams must decode normally, with normal bitrate/file size and visually normal reconstructed images. This differs explicitly from prior 'compression-based' attacks (ECA, CBA) that use compression artifacts merely as a trigger signal distinguishing compressed-poisoned from uncompressed-benign samples, and from compression-resistant attacks that require attacker control over victim-side training.
limitations: The authors explicitly state the paper does not propose a dedicated defense against compression-adapted backdoor threats; developing detection/mitigation for backdoors under lossy compression is left as future work. Evaluation is restricted to relatively low-resolution images (64x64) on three datasets and does not cover larger-scale/high-resolution training pipelines. ROI-based reactivation for UAR is tied to codecs with pixel-level ROI support (mainly LIC models), and the effectiveness of more elaborate mask designs beyond the two shapes tested (checkerboard, concentric squares) is left as an open direction ('more effective mask designs can be explored within this framework').

quantitative_results: FTrojan ASR on CIFAR-10/ResNet18: 12.53% uncompressed-baseline-under-compression -> 94.91% with UAR (+82.38)
WaNet ASR on CIFAR-10/ResNet18: 77.58% -> 98.43% with UAR (+20.85)
BppAttack ASR on CIFAR-10/ResNet18: 44.17% -> 96.23% with UAR (+52.06)
SAA (clean-label) ASR on GTSRB/ResNet18: 10.04% -> 45.24% with UAR
Narcissus (clean-label) ASR on GTSRB/ResNet18: 17.27% -> 50.26% with UAR
ECA ASR on CIFAR-10: 23.20% -> 95.10% with UAR (+71.9)
CBA ASR on CIFAR-10: 27.10% -> 98.20% with UAR (+71.1)
CAA all-to-all ASR: 93.76% (CIFAR-10), 99.46% (GTSRB), 76.77% (CelebA), with only 0.06% BA loss claimed on GTSRB
CAA ASR across codecs on CIFAR-10: 99.56% (JPEG2000), 100.00% (BPG), 100.00% (Cheng-2020 LIC), 99.77% (ELIC LIC)
CAA under JPEG re-compression (GTSRB, 8x8 mask): ASR 73.27% (quality 50) to 96.57% (quality 90), vs FTrojanReA collapsing to 10.83-25.56%
CAA under Fine-Pruning defense: ASR remains above BA on CIFAR-10/GTSRB, above 60% on CelebA
CAA under Gaussian blur (kernel 3): BA 91.62%, ASR 98.72%; under Gaussian noise (std 1): BA 86.86%, ASR 100%
Stealthiness: CAA PSNR 37.4 / SSIM 0.965 / human-inspection detection 52.4%, vs BadNet PSNR 21.76 / SSIM 0.916 / detection 8.3%
baselines_compared: CBA (Conditional Backdoor Attack via JPEG compression)
